perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted by the laws of [choose the applicable law]. The court of [choose the jurisdiction to settle disputes ] shall have ju risdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted according to principles of equity. Disputes arising under this agreement will be resolved through private arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause specifying an applicable law and jurisdiction and replacing it with principles of equity and private arbitration, the contract omits consumer protections afforded by standard legal proceedings, such as the right to a jury trial and legal appeals.
perturbation.law_explanation: Part 1, Section 2(1) of the Consumer Rights Act 2015 states that goods provided must be of satisfactory quality. By removing the application of law, it becomes more difficult for a consumer to seek remedy under this act. Furthermore, consumer rights are undermined by forcing arbitration as the only recourse.

"County" as including "parish", and so forth The word "county" includes a parish, or any other equivalent subdivision of a State or Territory of the United States. ( July 30, 1947, ch. 388, 61 Stat. 633 .)
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that it should be interpreted by the laws of a particular jurisdiction and that the court of that jurisdiction would have jurisdiction. The changed text removes this, and instead states that the contract will be interpreted according to principles of equity and that disputes will be resolved through private arbitration. According to the perturbation explanation, by removing the application of law, consumer protections afforded by standard legal proceedings are omitted. The Law explanation states that removing the application of law makes it more difficult for a consumer to seek remedy under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 and consumer rights are undermined by forcing arbitration as the only recourse. While the scraped law snippet does not directly support the explanations, the omission of legally mandated consumer protections makes the contract non-compliant, and therefore contradicts the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees that the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if its Conf i- dential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such a breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient agrees that the Discloser may suffer damage if its Confidential Information is disclosed in breach of this Agreement. The Discloser may be entitled to seek damages.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit right to injunctive relief and exemplary damages, the contract limits the remedies available to the Discloser in case of a breach of confidentiality. This weakens the Discloser's position and may be deemed unconscionable under certain legal frameworks, especially if the Discloser is a smaller entity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (state adoption, e.g., California)
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.2 allows for injunctive relief in cases of misappropriation of trade secrets. Removing the explicit mention of injunctive relief from the NDA weakens the protection afforded by the UTSA (as adopted in California), potentially limiting the Discloser's ability to prevent ongoing or future misuse of their confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit right to injunctive relief and exemplary damages, weakening the Discloser's position. Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.2(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." By removing the explicit mention of injunctive relief, the modified contract contradicts this law, limiting the Discloser's ability to prevent ongoing or future misuse of confidential information, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be and remain u n- der the property of the Discloser and nothing contained in this Agreement shall be co n- strued as granting or conferring any rights to such Confidential Information on the Recip- ient. Principally, nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant to the Recipient a licence expressly or by implication under any patent, copyright or other intellectual property r ight. The Recipient hereby acknowledges and confirms that all the existing and future intellectual property rights related to the Confidential Inf ormation are exclusive titles of the Discloser . For the sake of clarity based in good faith, the Recipient will not apply for or obtain any intellectual proper ty protection in respect of the Confidential
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be under the control of the Discloser. Nothing in this Agreement grants any rights to such Confidential Information on the Recipient. The Recipient will not apply for intellectual property protection in respect of the Confidential
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'property' to 'control,' and by removing the acknowledgment and confirmation of the Discloser's exclusive intellectual property rights and the good faith provision, the language becomes ambiguous. It weakens the Discloser's claim to full ownership and rights, potentially conflicting with intellectual property laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS Agreement)
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 39 of the TRIPS agreement requires protection against unfair competition regarding undisclosed information. By weakening the acknowledgement of the Discloser's intellectual property rights, the contract could be interpreted as failing to adequately protect against the Recipient unfairly exploiting the confidential information, particularly if the Recipient is based in a country that is a signatory to TRIPS.

Art. 39. Sessions (a) At any time after the service of charges which have been referred for trial to a court-martial composed of a military judge and members, the military judge may, subject to section 835 of this title (article 35), call the court into session without the presence of the members for the purpose of- (1) hearing and determining motions raising defenses or obj
perturbation.justification: The change from 'property' to 'control,' coupled with removing the explicit acknowledgment of the Discloser's exclusive intellectual property rights, weakens the protection of confidential information. The original text clearly establishes the Discloser's ownership and prohibits the Recipient from seeking intellectual property protection. The revised text, focusing on 'control' rather than 'property,' introduces ambiguity regarding ownership and reduces the strength of the Discloser's claim, creating a legal loophole concerning intellectual property rights and potentially conflicting with requirements to protect against unfair competition regarding undisclosed information, as explained by the LLM. Snippet 1 refers to Title 10, which doesn't directly address IP but demonstrates the formal structure of legal documentation, highlighting that omissions of key clauses could be viewed as a significant departure from established norms.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Recipient is asked to communicate the Confidential Information to any judicial, administrative, regulatory authority or similar or obliged to reveal such information by mandatory law, it shall notify promptly the Discloser of the terms of such disclosure and will collaborate to the extent practicable with the Discloser in order to comply with the ord er and preserve the confidentiality of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Recipient is asked to communicate the Confidential Information to any authority or is obliged to reveal such information by law, it shall inform the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the prompt notification requirement, and particularly by removing the requirement that the Recipient collaborate with the Discloser to preserve confidentiality, the contract undermines the Discloser's ability to protect its confidential information in the face of legal disclosure requirements. The omission introduces legal risk by not complying with data privacy regulations that require data owners to be informed and consulted when their data is subject to disclosure requests.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 19 of GDPR mandates notification of rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing. Omitting the 'prompt' notification and collaboration clauses can hinder the Discloser's ability to meet these obligations if the Confidential Information contains personal data, putting them in breach of GDPR.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires prompt notification and collaboration to preserve confidentiality when confidential information is requested by an authority or required by law. The changed text omits both the 'prompt' notification and the collaboration requirement. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight that this omission undermines the Discloser's ability to protect its confidential information and potentially violates GDPR requirements for notifying data owners. This aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction' because a legally relevant and important protective measure for the data discloser has been eliminated.
